turns.content: Why does this even work this way? I thought every shared state in Rust required a Mutex or RwLock, but the tutorial just showed me using an Actor pattern with mpsc channels and it seems to handle everything without any locking primitives at all.

That's a great observation. The core idea is that in this architecture, each actor owns its own data. When an actor needs to send information out, it uses a channel like tokio::sync::mpsc or std::sync::mpsc, and the receiver side simply consumes messages without ever needing to access the sender's internal state directly.
But what if two different actors try to update a shared counter at the same time? Doesn't that still require some kind of synchronization mechanism like tokio::sync::Mutex?

Exactly. If you need to modify shared mutable state from multiple threads or async tasks, you absolutely should use something like tokio::sync::Mutex or std::sync::Mutex depending on whether you're in an async context or blocking code.
So the tutorial is avoiding these primitives entirely? Is it safe to just pass data through channels and never touch shared memory?

The pattern works because the actors don't share mutable state directly. Instead, they communicate via message passing using types like tokio::sync::mpsc channel or std::sync::mpsc. The sender puts a message into the queue and the receiver takes it out—there's no concurrent write to shared memory.
Wait, so if I want to pass complex objects like Vec<i32> or HashMap<String, bool> through these channels, won't that cause data races?

No. As long as the receiver only reads from the channel and never mutates the object it received directly, there's no race condition. The ownership semantics of Rust ensure that once an actor takes a value out of a channel, it owns that value until it drops or passes it along.
Okay I think I finally get it. But what about watch channels? When do I use those instead of mpsc?

Watch channels are useful when you want to observe state changes without explicitly sending messages every time something updates. They're part of the async ecosystem and work well with tokio's runtime, but they still follow the same principle: no direct shared mutation.
